agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This is an information item only — no bylaw or regulation is being proposed. The report provides data on homelessness in the region, which may inform future policy decisions that could affect businesses, particularly those in the Hotel & Accommodation and Retail sectors. No speakers are allowed during this agenda item per the Procedure Bylaw.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants totaling $5,509,770 for 85 cultural organizations. The grants cover 'Operating' (Institutions: $2,828,160; Annual: $709,760; Multi-year: $1,897,550/year for 2026-2028) and 'Cultural Spaces' (including a $75,000 grant for the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society and a $728,000 one-time grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades). Recommendations A to C, E, and G require at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' under section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a Vancouver arts or culture organization, watch for the council vote on April 14, 2026 — this could affect your funding.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on awarding 19 Community Sport Event Grants totaling $89,000 to local organizations for spring 2026 events. The grants range from $1,000 to $5,000 per event. A two-thirds majority vote is required under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. A second intake will open in Fall 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council vote on April 14, 2026 — you may be able to apply for a grant in the Fall 2026 intake.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a recommendation to set the 2026 general purpose tax share at approximately 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential, with no tax shift proposed. The total general purpose tax levy is $1,224,250,000. Tax rates will be calculated based on this distribution and the revised assessment roll, which decreased by $28.1 billion (5.9%) year-over-year. The recommendation maintains the existing tax share split from 2025.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the April 14, 2026 council meeting or submit comments to have input on the tax distribution vote.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026 property tax distribution

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council received the 2025 Property Tax Exemptions report. In 2025, $60.9 billion of properties (12% of the Assessment Roll) were exempt, resulting in $335.1 million in forgone general purpose taxes, partially offset by $34.8 million in compensating payments-in-lieu of taxes. Any net forgone taxes are redistributed to other taxpayers. Statutory exemptions apply to Crown lands, City property, charitable institutions, learning institutions, hospitals, churches, emergency shelters, and others. Permissive exemptions are discretionary by Council.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy that creates new rules for building and development in Vancouver's coastal flood risk areas. The policy leverages land-use authority and redevelopment opportunities to incrementally improve long-term flood resilience. City-built structural flood measures outside redevelopment will be considered only in specific situations, focused on preserving City facilities and core service delivery, subject to financial capacity.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date (April 14, 2026) and consider submitting comments if you own property or plan to develop in a coastal flood risk area.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Coastal Flood Management Policy

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation Annual Report 2025
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on approving the Vancouver Heritage Foundation's 2025 Annual Report and a $152,375 grant from the 2026 Operating Budget for the first year of a three-year operating agreement (2026-2028). Approval requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The grant funds staffing and resources for educational programming, activities, and conservation work supporting the City's heritage.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposal to designate 13 public plazas for alcohol consumption under a new 3-year program cycle (2026-2029), plus a 2-year extension for the Drinker's Lounge parklet at 111 Princess St. The program offers seasonal or year-round participation, with permissible hours tied to washroom availability. No health or safety issues have been reported since the program began in 2020.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if your business is near a proposed plaza site.

key_dates.label: Council vote on proposed public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on endorsing a $200,000 grant application to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would be used to redesign approval processes for large-scale housing developments, including creating reusable guides, templates, and service blueprints to simplify regulations and reduce delays. The grant would cover 100% of eligible costs for the Development, Building & Licensing department.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Vancouver.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning condition changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on 11 housing rezoning condition changes under the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP), a two-year pilot (Feb 1, 2026 – Dec 15, 2027). Eligible mid- and high-rise BMR projects may adjust affordability from a 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents to rates not exceeding city-wide averages for all purpose-built rentals (up to 25% discount vs 2024 market rents). Projects must be in a community plan area, be 9+ storeys, have fewer than 10 existing rental units, be under 8,000 sq m, and not yet have a Stage 1 building permit (with limited exceptions for permits issued Dec 15, 2025 – Mar 20, 2026).
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you have an eligible BMR project, review the RDRP criteria and prepare to negotiate amended Housing Agreements before the pilot ends Dec 15, 2027.

key_dates.label: Rental Development Relief Program pilot ends

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a text amendment to CD-1 (839) Bylaw No. 13493 for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment would increase the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5 and change the housing from for-profit affordable rental (leased by a non-profit, with 30% of units for households below HILs at ≤30% of income) to 100% secured market rental. The draft bylaw requires at least 35% of units to have 2+ bedrooms and sets a maximum building height of 22.3 m.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect development rules for these properties.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for 4088 Granville Street. A draft bylaw would change the zoning from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, increasing the maximum floor space ratio from 0.5 to 1.2 and maximum building height from 11.5 m (38 ft.) to 13.4 m (44 ft.) to permit three three-storey residential rental buildings containing 31 units. At least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming item on the council agenda.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The plan would create a CD-1 zone for a six-storey mixed-use building with 82 secured market rental units and commercial at grade (minimum 594.9 m²). A key condition: if a stage one building permit is not issued within two years of approval, at least 20% of residential floor area must revert to below-market rental units. The proposal also includes changes to the Sign Bylaw and Noise Control Bylaw. This is not yet approved.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will direct staff to consult on proposed Amenity Cost Charge (ACC) bylaw and Bill 16 updates to density bonus and inclusionary zoning rules. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square maintenance costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. A 'local improvement project' is a way for a city to pay for upgrades or upkeep in a specific area by billing the properties that benefit from the work. If approved, this bylaw would mean owners of affected properties would have to pay a share of the maintenance costs for these alleys and the square.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area and watch for the council vote on this bylaw.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge certain property owners in the East Hastings area for the costs of a shared parking project in 2025. The bylaw would 'assess real property' — meaning it would add a special fee or tax on specific properties to pay for the collective parking project. This is not yet approved; council will decide at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — for example, requiring a certain number of affordable rental units for a set number of years. This proposed bylaw has not been approved yet; council will decide at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change a Housing Agreement for three properties in Burnaby: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules for how the housing is used — for example, keeping some units affordable. This proposed amendment would update the terms of that existing agreement. The decision has not been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on amending CD-1 bylaw at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would change the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zoning rules for properties at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. A 'bylaw' is a local law passed by the city council. The current CD-1 bylaw (No. 6254) sets specific rules for what can be built and how the land can be used at that location. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'C-2A Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, the new rules would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels (under a certain size) more quickly, without needing a full rezoning process for each project. The new C-2A district would replace the existing C-2, C-2B, C-2C, and C-2C1 districts on certain parcels. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet. Council will consider the report at an upcoming meeting, and there may be a public hearing before a final vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council calls on senior governments to address homelessness with new supports
minutes_briefs.summary: Council voted unanimously to receive the 2025 Homeless Count report for information and to formally call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to take several actions. These include increasing investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence that contributes to homelessness. Council also asks senior governments to re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre, expand mental health and substance use treatment services across Metro Vancouver and BC, strengthen discharge planning from health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent people from being discharged into homelessness, and provide support to local governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports. This is a formal request from Burnaby to higher levels of government — no new local rules or costs for businesses are created by this decision.
minutes_briefs.industries: Healthcare, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Labour & Employment
minutes_briefs.source_body:
1. 2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
April 1, 2026

The General Manager, Arts, Culture and Community Services along with staff from Arts, Culture and Community Services, provided a presentation and responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Dominato
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  
a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;
b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre; 
c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community; 
d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and
e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering.  

amended

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Maloney

THAT the words “and housing with supports” be added to the end of e.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11512)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11513) with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  
a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;
b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre; 
c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community; 
d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and
e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $9.5M in cultural grants for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Council approved $9.5 million in cultural grants for 2026. The funding includes operating grants for 83 arts organizations, multi-year grants for 52 groups, and special project grants. An additional $25,000 was added for the Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society. A one-time grant of $728,000 was approved for the Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials removal and accessibility upgrades. The Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society received $250,000 for a feasibility study. Council also delegated authority to finalize grant agreements.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Eligible organizations should prepare to sign grant agreements with the City.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
2. 2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
March 30, 2026

Staff from Arts, Culture and Community Services responded to questions.

Council heard from two speakers who spoke in opposition to the report recommendations.

MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Zhou

C. D. THAT Council approve amended conditions as further described on page 6 under the section titled “Recommendations D to F: Making Space for Arts and Culture Grant” of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the disbursement of the Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of $175,000 approved by Council on December 10, 2025, (Council Report RTS 18091) to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study.

(Councillor Bligh absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY AND A to C, E and G BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11515).

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding - Institutions grants totalling $2,828,160 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the five organizations listed in Section A of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),”  for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those five organizations to $3,783,680.

THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding – Annual grants totalling $709,760 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the 26 organizations listed in Section B of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those 26 organizations to $1,097,000, and that an additional $25,000 be added to this grant stream from reserves and allocated to The Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society of Vancouver.

THAT Council approve a Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of an additional $75,000 to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support additional site assessment needs for the development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve.    

F. THAT, subject to the approval of E above, Council approve an increase to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan and the Multi-year Capital Project Budget for the Cultural Spaces program by $75,000, to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 19 community sport event grants worth $89,000
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved 19 community sport event grants totaling $89,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants are funded from the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget. Each grant recipient must agree to the program's terms and conditions, and no legal rights or obligations are created until the grant agreement letter is signed and delivered. Council also deemed non-registered charity organizations listed in the report as contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or welfare of the city, as allowed under the Vancouver Charter.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If your organization is listed in Table 1 of the report, sign and return the grant agreement letter to the City to receive the funds.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
3. Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
March 10, 2026

A. THAT Council approve 19 new Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 as outlined in the body of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” to each organization listed in Table 1 of the same report, under the column entitled “Organization” and for the amount set out beside their name in the column entitled “Grant Recommended”; source of funding is the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget.

B. THAT the grants in A above be subject to each grant recipient agreeing to the terms and conditions of the community sport hosting grant program as generally outlined in this report and otherwise satisfactory to the Director, Tourism and Destination Events and the City Solicitor. 

C. THAT no legal rights or obligations are created by the approval of the A and B above unless and until the grant agreement letter is executed and delivered by the grant recipient. 

D. THAT pursuant to the Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter, the organizations listed in Table 1 of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” which are not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency are deemed by Council to be an organization contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or welfare of the City. 

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND A BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11523)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council adopts Coastal Flood Management Policy
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has adopted a new Coastal Flood Management Policy that outlines the city's role in planning and managing both structural measures (like sea walls) and non-structural measures (like land-use rules) to protect against coastal flooding and sea level rise. A proposed amendment to also create a multi-government task force for the False Creek floodplain was defeated, so the policy was approved as originally presented without that additional study.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics
minutes_briefs.source_body:
6. Coastal Flood Management Policy
March 17, 2026

Staff from Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and Legal Services responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy,” which articulates the City’s role and supports planning, implementation, and management of structural and non-structural coastal flood management measures in Vancouver.

carried

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Orr

A. THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy.”

B.  THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and inter-agency task force for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of: 
1. identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise; 
2. assessing whether a coordinated, area-wide structural flood management approach would be more effective and cost-efficient than fragmented site-by-site measures; 
3. identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation in respect of Granville Island, the Department of National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place, Providence Health Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro, Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as staff may determine; 
4. identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required to support any future funding strategy or senior-government engagement; and
5.         reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council decisions on capital commitments.

C.  THAT the report back in B above also assesses whether similar multi-jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek-specific approach is warranted.

LOST (Vote No. 11516)
(Councillors Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11517) with Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle bylaw changes for public plaza drinking
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted unanimously to approve, in principle, amendments to the City Land Regulation By-law (a rule that controls which public spaces are allowed for certain activities) to allow alcohol consumption in certain public plazas from 2026 to 2028. The bylaw will be formally enacted later, based on a list of approved plaza sites. This decision is final and will let people drink alcohol in designated public plazas during the program period.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved amendments to housing rezoning conditions for multiple Burnaby developments
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved amendments to housing rezoning conditions for several developments listed in the report. The changes will be integrated into the respective Housing Agreements, which are legal contracts between the city and property owners that set out requirements for affordable housing, community amenities, or other conditions tied to the rezoning. The approval is subject to each property owner agreeing to the updated conditions.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 text amendment for 5590 Victoria Drive rental housing
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The change increases the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5 and switches the project from for-profit affordable rental housing (leased by a non-profit, with 30% of units for low-income households) to secured market rental housing. The developer must still enter into a Housing Agreement with the city before the new bylaw takes effect.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 31 rental units at 4088 Granville Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved in principle a rezoning for 4088 Granville Street. The property will change from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This allows the owner to build three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units. minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved new C-2A zone to fast-track rental housing and hotels
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a new commercial zoning district called C-2A. This decision creates a new set of rules for certain commercial properties. The goal is to make it faster and easier to build rental housing and small hotels in these areas. Council voted to change several existing commercial zones (C-2, C-2B, C-2C, and C-2C1) to the new C-2A district. They also updated related bylaws, including rules for parking, signs, noise, and subdivision, to match the new zone. This is a completed decision — the new rules are now in effect.

This decision is final and will determine how the total property tax levy is split between homeowners and businesses for the 2026 tax year.
